ground_truth.title: Investment Strategy Review Request, 2024 Version
ground_truth.author: changingtimes
ground_truth.main_content:
changingtimes
Sat Feb 24, 2024 12:41 pm

Hello again--

I think it's time for a new advice request/portfolio check. It's now been seven years(!) since my husband died, and I have moved/am moving a lot of chess pieces.

The event that's making me come back for a new round of input is that I am selling the home that DH and I bought 29 years ago. It is no longer my primary home, but that changed happened in 2023 and so I will still get the $250k CG exemption. Depending on the sale price (and keeping in mind the stepped-up cost basis), I think I will manage to either stay just below or end up just above the $250k line, and so don't expect to pay a ton of capital gains. (Have done a whole lot of capital improvements in 29 years!) I am giving up a 2.5% mortgage on $360k, but my mental health is suffering as long as I stay in this house in this neighborhood. And I'm so tired of upkeep on a 120-year-old home (and "just hire people" isn't as easy as it ought to be) that I'm only in for less than six months out of the year. I'm just done and ready to go.

I'm expecting to net about $700k from this sale, and given the market where I am, I don't expect it to take long. I have no intention of buying another place anytime soon, though in the fall I will probably be renting a (pricey) apartment in a different part of the same east coast HCOL area as the house I'm selling. In the meantime I will be living in my house in Wyoming (see below). I adore Wyoming, but living there full-time is not quite yet on my agenda, especially with an elderly father who currently lives near me (and who I would be moving even closer to after this sale).

I have no children, and while I have an estate plan for how to distribute what's left when I am gone, I am not making any effort to set anything aside.

I am still working fully remote 32 hours a week, with a salary right around $100k. I receive a $11k/year pension from DH. My own pension at 65 will be about $45k (no COLA), or the more likely scenario is that I can take $40k of it if I retire after 61 (rule of 90) and the rest at 65. DH's SS survivor benefits would be about $30k if I took it at 61 (don't want to take it before I stop working). I would wait on my own SS until age 70, which would be around $48k in current dollars.

I would consider my base yearly spend to be around $100k once the pricey apartment is added to my lineup.

State of Residence: Wyoming
I bought a one story condo that is half of a duplex in 2022, and the remaining mortgage is about $168k at 4.25% (PITI + condo fee = $1,700/month). House is probably worth about $500k.
I also own 50% of a couple hundred acres of undeveloped land, so $300k is the value to me. (currently renting it out for a pittance to the neighbor to run his cattle on to get the agricultural exemption on property tax). I imagine we'll sell it at some point in a decade or so.

Tax Rate: 22% or 24% Fed, depending on how much I withdraw from DH's IRA for spending money or to convert to Roth; 0% state

Asset Allocation: Currently about 67/33.

Age: 57. Will hit 59.5 in early 2026.

Current Retirement Assets

Taxable at Vanguard
$0.00 right now, $700k likely by late spring
Settlement fund is VMFXX

401k at Vanguard
52% Vanguard Target 2030 (VTHRX), current value about $1.05m

Spousal Inherited IRA at Vanguard
21% Vanguard Wellington Admiral (VWENX), current value about $400k
This is the account I've been withdrawing from to fill out my cash flow (no penalties, just income tax)

Roth IRA at Vanguard
15% Vanguard Total Stock Market Admiral (VTSAX), current value about $300k

Traditional IRA at Vanguard
12% Vanguard Wellesley Admiral (VWIAX), current value about $230k
This is the account that I roll money into from the inherited IRA to in order to then make Roth conversions

Health Savings Account
1% Vanguard Index 500 (VFIAX), current value about $21k

Contributions

New annual Contributions
$6k to 401k, $3k employer match
$3,850 to HSA, $750 employer contribution

Available funds

Funds available in 401(k)
Vanguard target date funds
Vanguard Wellington Admiral (VWENX), 0.17%
Vanguard Wellesley Admiral (VWIAX), 0.16%
Vanguard Windsor Admiral (VWNEX), 0.28%
Sequoia (SEQUX), 1.09% ER
Vanguard Growth Index Fund Institutional (VIGIX), 0.04%
Vanguard 500 Institutional Investor (VINIX), 0.04%
Vanguard Total International Stock Index Fund Institutional (VTSNX), 0.08%
Vanguard Mid-Cap Index Institutional (VMCIX), 0.04%
Vanguard Small-Cap Index Institutional (VSIIX), 0.06%
Vanguard Short-Term Investment Grade Fund Institutional (VFSIX), 0.07%
Vanguard Money Market Fund (VMFXX), 0.11%

Questions:

1. With the $700k influx, I would probably cut my asset allocation to 60/40 from 67/33? I feel like I have enough to be a bit aggressive, but not crazy.

2. How should I invest the $700k? I would probably want $100-200k in short-term available funds, and would be okay with a longer-term CD for more of it (especially since rates might start to drop soon). But am open to all suggestions.

3. How should I rebalance everything else, especially keeping in mind how my base tax situation will change with such a large influx of money into my taxable account?

4. Should I consider paying off the $168k mortgage at 4.25%, or wait until returns on money markets/CDs/etc drop below that?

5. Should I max out Roth conversions, up to 24% and even 25%? My long-term guestimations are that I would probably be in the 25% bracket in retirement.

I tend to feel like I've "won the game," but since I'll be aging solo there will probably be some extra associated costs with that, unless I'm gored by a bull or hit in the head with a stray golf ball.

Thanks as always for any insights. Bogleheads has been my guiding light through these past years of change in my life, though I do not have the aversion to spending money that some do!

invest4
Sat Feb 24, 2024 5:12 pm

Some initial comments:

Your Questions:

1. I think as long as you know yourself and can handle the volatility, this is fine and reasonable and not a game changer for your portfolio. Curious how you arrived at 67/33 vs 65/35 for example.

2. I would consider having 2-3 years of living expenses in cash / CDs. (200-300K based upon your 100K annual) to enable you to smooth out any bumps along the way (sequence of returns risk, bad down market, etc.) and invest the rest per your asset allocation.

3. I have never had a taxable account...will let others chime in how that may be taken into account.

4. This is a never ending topic of debate on our forum. Some people really hate debt and want it gone as soon as possible, while others like the benefits it provides (hedge against inflation, liquidity, leverage for additional investments, etc.). I would be inclined to let it ride a bit and see how interest rates shake out and how you feel about it.

5. Again, will defer to others who have more experience in this area...not a topic on my radar as yet.

I am very sorry for the loss of your husband.

I think you are doing great and in good position for when you decide to stop earning a paycheck.

Best wishes.

bonesly
Sat Feb 24, 2024 5:30 pm

This doesn't seem unreasonable. Bill Bernstein is quoted as saying "when you've won the game stop playing" (with the money you really need for retirement).

Having some cash in taxable is fine for short-term expenses. For anything longer term (supplementing your current retirement portfolio), you'd want the taxable account to hold stocks (e.g., Total US Stock Index) to adhere to Tax-Efficient Fund Placement. To reach your total portfolio AA of 60/40, you'd add bonds (e.g., Total US Bond Index or a ladder of individual T-Notes) in a percentage that will get you to your target in the tax-deferred accounts (not taxable, not Roth).

Paying of the mortgage is a guaranteed rate-of-return of 4.25%. You didn't say how many years are remaining on the term, but if it's more than 3-5 years, it seems unlikely that you'll find a CD paying that much for a 5-year fixed term (or longer). There's also the tax-burden of 22% on the interest of a CD, so effectively you need find something that's paying 5.45% to break even with paying the mortgage, and higher still to make that more attractive than pre-paying the mortgage.

If you hold discretionary funds in taxable, that might be better used to the pay the taxes due on Roth conversions rather than pre-paying the mortgage (see #5 below). You might have enough to do both.

$11K from DH pension
$45K your pension (no COLA) @ 65
$48K your SocSec @ 70
----
$141K total from "non-portfolio sources"

Portfolio of about $2,700K worth another $108K/yr @ 4% initial draw (if you could draw from it right now). At $249K/yr, I would concur you've "won the game." Without running a Monte Carlo to analyze a glide-path AA over time, step-changes in spending as mortgages are paid, or long-term care costs increasing later in life, I'd say you're set. Congratulations!

nhs76
Sat Feb 24, 2024 5:43 pm

You didn't mention this, but with most things buttoned down as well as you have done, I would suggest also having a good insurance agent to advise on the nuances of your situation: two states, rental property, agricultural land, etc. Were it me, I would want insurance policies in place including an umbrella policy with a fairly high limit. Good luck with the house sale.

changingtimes
Sat Feb 24, 2024 6:09 pm

Thank you! I learned about umbrella policies from Bogleheads, and I do have a $2m policy in place.

changingtimes
Sat Feb 24, 2024 6:15 pm

I think I was briefly at 65/35 and then just haven't bothered to rebalance.

I was thinking I can probably get away with $200k since I am still working.

Thank you on both counts, and for all of your responses to my questions.

Best wishes.

changingtimes
Sat Feb 24, 2024 6:37 pm

Bonesly, thank you for your detailed responses, I appreciate the time you took and also the thoughtfulness. Some replies:

After I posted I realized that I would probably want to go with stocks in the taxable with anything non short term. Will be fun to game out where to muck with the allocations.

Thank you for doing that math. I can never wrap my head around it! The mortgage isn't even two years old. I have no real problem with debt per se, and the notion of using that money to pay the tax in Roth conversions instead of paying off the mortgage is a good one.

Again, I really appreciate your responses. This is certainly not the way I expected like to go, and there will always be a huge hole in my heart, but I am so much luckier than most women in my position. We saved well while still living well.
